frontmatter.title: Virtual Control using State Space Matrices

children.value: Virtual Control using State Space Matrices

If no data acquisition hardware is available, it can still be advantageous to run Rattlesnake in a “virtual” mode by simulating responses to virtual forces using some kind of model.  This would allow users of the software to develop new control laws and environments without risking damage to potentially expensive test hardware if the control law or environment is not implemented correctly.  Additionally, synthetic control can be used to determine proper test parameters prior to the actual test occurring, which can help determine if a given test is feasible or not.
Rattlesnake is able to integrate linear time-invariant equations of motion using general state space matrices.  The general form for state space equations of motion is

children.label: eq:state_eoms
children.html: x˙=Ax+Bu\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}x˙=Ax+Bu

children.label: eq:output_eoms
children.html: y=Cx+Du\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}y=Cx+Du

children.value: This chapter describes the setup and implementation details for running a synthetic control problem using state space matrices.  For a complete example of using this type of control, see
Setting up the Channel Table for Virtual Control using State Space Matrics

children.label: Setting up the Channel Table for Virtual Control using State Space Matrics Section 8.1

children.value: Required channel table inputs for virtual control using state space matrices are minimal, as the

children.html: C\mathbf{C}C
D\mathbf{D}D

children.value: matrices effectively define the measured degrees of freedom.  Still users are encouraged to fill out the channel table as much as possible for test documentation’s sake.  The only required parameters for virtual control are have an entry in the Physical Device column of the channel table.  The authors recommend simply using the word “Virtual” to reinforce the fact that these are not real hardware channels, but instead virtual channels that exist only in software.  The excitation channels must also have an entry in the Feedback Device column.  The authors recommend simply using the word “Input” to make it clear that these channels are input channels to the system.  One important consideration is that the number of channels in the channel table must be the same as the number of output degrees of freedom in

children.html: y\mathbf{y}y

children.value: (and therefore the number of rows of matrices

children.html: C\mathbf{C}C
D\mathbf{D}D

children.value: ), and they must also be ordered identically.  Similarly, the number of input channels to the system specified in the channel table must be the same as the number of input degrees of freedom in

children.value: (and therefore the number of columns of matrices

hardware device is selected, Rattlesnake will bring up a file dialog box that will allow the user to specify a Numpy or Matlab file from which the state space matrices will be loaded.  This file should contain fields
containing the appropriately sized matrices.  An additional parameter will appear in the
tab that allows the user to specify an
amount, as the integration time steps should generally be finer than the sample rate of the controller.  A value of 10 is typically sufficient for integration of a linear system using
model is created from the loaded matrices which are used for integration using the
Excitation signals coming into the state space virtual hardware are buffered to enable different read and write sizes.  The excitation signals will be delivered to the hardware based on the specified
tab of the controller.  The virtual hardware will then remove an amount of excitation signal from the buffer based on the specified
, as the acquisition task will perform the integration over time blocks equivalent to

function is used to integrate the state space model.  When the acquisition is started, the state of the integration is initialized to zero.  For subsequent integrations, the initial state is set to the final state of the previous integration.  The excitation signals are taken from the force buffer discussed previously.  Note that integration is performed at the rate specified by the
of the data acquisition system multiplied by the
amount.  After the integration is performed, the output is downsampled by the
amount to return to the sample rate of the controller.  For subsequent integration blocks, the initial conditions of the integration are equivalent to the final value of the last integration block.
